summary: Reis argues that AI tools are making software trivially easy to build, transforming it from something you rent (SaaS) into something disposable you generate on demand. While this doesn't kill all software companies — those with deep data gravity, network effects, and integration ecosystems retain their moats — vendors whose value proposition is merely a workflow wrapper or single feature are facing existential threat from millions of AI-empowered builders chipping away at their offerings.
key_points: Software is shifting from rented generic capabilities to generated specific ones, undermining the SaaS business model
Jevons Paradox applies: easier software creation leads to dramatically more software, not less
Vendors whose value is a UI wrapper on a database with business logic are most exposed to AI disruption
Companies with deep data gravity, network effects, and integration ecosystems have more runway than the market prices in
The friction between having an idea and solving the problem has never been lower, creating an era of abundance for builders
money_quotes: The assumption that SaaS was the golden goose was based on the idea that software was hard to build and easy to rent. That dynamic has flipped. Software is now easy to build, and increasingly hard to justify renting.
We are moving from an era of renting generic capabilities to generating specific ones.
If I can describe what your product does in two sentences, AI can probably build it.
Vendors are facing a potential Lilliputian attack of millions of people using AI to chip away at countless vendor flagship offerings from millions of tiny angles.
The tools have caught up with our imaginations, and it turns out our imaginations are insatiable.
key_insight: The SaaS model depended on software being hard to build and easy to rent, but AI has inverted that equation — making software disposable and generated on demand while triggering a Jevons Paradox where easier creation drives exponentially more software into existence.
